h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: I doubled the side, so saying the area doubles still feels right.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner is progressively conceding the four-square count while still hedging toward the original doubling intuition.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The line has stopped settling it. It keeps making a strip look like the whole square. Start with the claim, not the answer. Use two tiles first. Cover the doubled square, no gaps, no overhang, while the outside edges stay two old lengths across and two old lengths down. Stop where the grid refuses.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: After L3 shows the rotated grid line still fails to dislodge the strip illusion, T3 abandons the visual-line strategy entirely and shifts to a physical tiling task that forces the learner to enact the contradiction rather than observe it.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The tiles have settled the figure; they have not settled the sentence. [turns the grid sheet over and leaves only the four loose tiles visible] No grid now. Make one sentence that can pass with only these tiles in view: it has to name two across and two down, or it is only talking about the strip.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T4 directly responds to L4's tile-based concession by removing the grid entirely and redirecting the task from counting to articulation, adapting the evidence standard to match the learner's newly tile-grounded frame.
